editorialSummary.text: Contemporary restaurant & bar offering Indian fusion fare in laid-back digs with sports on TV.

text.text:
Indian- Italian fusion that’s just a flavor bomb I didn’t know I needed to try.This place is a bit unassuming as it’s a restaurant connected to a Comfort Inn with a crappy parking lot. But the interior was very clean and well decorated. They had a full bar, big screen tvs all round, a very fun and hip ambiance the moment you step inside. (Something I love about dining out is the magic in the food service industry and that magic is definitely here.)

We got the Malai Rigatoni and the Green Tikka Masala Pijja. The first bite of the rigatoni blew my mind- it was so flavorful and so good. It did have a bit of heat like a medium spice, but a very enjoyable spice level. It was creamy, spicy, Smokey and filled with umami. I didn’t care too much for the green tikka pijja; it was a good pizza overall, it just was missing a note for me. I think next time I would just try some more of their pasta options.

Food was tasty and came out quickly, but the biggest show stopper of Pijja Palace was the service. Our waitress Anna Lee was such a sweetheart. She was attentive, caring, and informative. I would come back for her service alone. Not only the delicious food, but, Anna Lee’s service made my dinner experience a very memorable one.

text.text:
The malai rigatoni tastes like vodka pasta with a little spice, kinda not seeing the indian flavor. But it was great. Getting a little of indian kashmiri pepper or a cayenne spice. Well cooked though!

The pizza was a pretty basic thin-crust pizza. It tasted like they had just added some green chutney on top, which was fine, but I didn’t really understand the hype. Overall though, the food was good and very filling. The rigatoni, however, was great and definitely the highlight.

text.text:
This was my second visit to Pijja Palace. It’s on Sunset Blvd, and coming from the South Bay it took about 40 minutes, so it’s a place I plan ahead for.

The menu is Indian-influenced comfort food—pizza, burgers, bar-style dishes—meant to be shared. We ordered a few items and the onion rings were especially good. The Indian-inspired pizzas were flavorful and filling, with sauces you can add depending on spice level. We also ordered the cheese chili toast, which tasted familiar and worked well with the rest of the menu. Drinks include Indian flavor notes - order the rose lemonade!

The server was very friendly, which made the experience easy and relaxed.

Parking can be tricky. There’s a small lot in front plus street parking. We made a lunch reservation, and going right when they open is a good strategy if you want to avoid parking stress.

I’d go back with friends—pizza and shared plates make it a fun group spot.

text.text:
We've been wanting to try Pijja Palace for a while now, and there's definitely been a lot of hype surrounding it!
The staff is very friendly, and the overall vibes are great. The place itself is cute, modern, and welcoming. There is limited parking available, though, so that's something to keep in mind. The food is essentially bar food with Indian flavors. The menu is small, but easy to navigate.
We tried the fries, onion rings, two types of wings, and two types of pizzas. Overall, the food was good, but my favorite part was definitely the pizza crust, it was amazing. The onion rings were pretty good too. The fries, however, weren't my favorite. They were heavily seasoned, which didn't quite hit the mark for me. Buffalo wings were quite tough for some reason. But the Hari Mirch Masala wings were very juicy. So i am not sure what went wrong there.
I also tried their margarita, which was tasty but honestly, not very strong.
Would I go back? Probably not, but it was still a good experience

text.text:
Ive been to LA many times and this is the place that I always come back to. The combination of Indian and Italian food might seem weird, but its executed in a way that makes your life better.

Try the pizzas. Try the pastas. Try the cocktails. Try it all.

Blessed be the immigrants that know how to blend our cultures in such a magnificent way.
